data.title: Goldman Sachs outlines path to $300B private credit target while accelerating cloud and data investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “In the first quarter, we delivered a very strong performance, generating net revenues of $17.2 billion, net earnings of $5.6 billion and earnings per share of $17.55.” (Chairman & CEO David Solomon) He also cited “a return on equity of 19.8% and an ROTE of 21.3%,” while pointing to “volatility increased meaningfully” tied to “AI-driven disruption,” “heightened uncertainty in parts of private credit,” and “the conflict in the Middle East.”
  • “In Global Banking & Markets, we delivered record quarterly revenues,” and in Asset & Wealth Management, “We generated $62 billion in long-term fee-based inflows.” (Chairman & CEO Solomon) He also said, “We are pleased to have closed the acquisition of Innovator in the second quarter,” adding it “adds an additional $31 billion in assets under supervision across a suite of over 170 ETF.”
  • “We are, therefore, accelerating our investments in cloud migration, and in the accuracy, completeness and timeliness of our data.” (Chief Financial Officer Denis Coleman) He linked this to AI enablement, saying the goal is “optimizing the deployment of AI solutions across the firm.”

Outlook

  • “We remain confident that over time, One GS 3.0 will drive stronger operating leverage, greater resilience and improved efficiency and returns.” (Chairman & CEO Solomon)
  • “We expect revenues for the rest of the year to run lower, in line with seasonal trends in the business.” (CFO Coleman) He attributed the Platform Solutions change to “the move of the Apple portfolio to held for sale.”
  • “For the full year, we expect a tax rate of approximately 20%.” (CFO Coleman)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s emphasis on a “highly constructive setup for 2026,” management language in Q1 leaned more toward uncertainty, with Solomon noting “the macro environment started to weigh on sentiment.”

Financial results

  • “In the first quarter, we delivered a very strong performance, generating net revenues of $17.2 billion, net earnings of $5.6 billion and earnings per share of $17.55.” (Chairman & CEO Solomon)
  • “Global Banking & Markets produced record revenues of $12.7 billion.” (CFO Coleman) He reported “Advisory revenues of $1.5 billion,” “FICC net revenues were $4 billion,” and “Equities net revenues were a record $5.3 billion.”
  • “Asset & Wealth Management revenues were $4.1 billion,” with “management and other fees…$3.1 billion,” and “firm-wide net interest income was $3.7 billion.” (CFO Coleman) He also cited “total quarterly operating expenses…$10.4 billion,” “an efficiency ratio of 60.5%,” and “non-compensation expenses…$5 billion.”
  • “Our Common Equity Tier 1 ratio was 12.5%…110 basis points above our current capital requirement of 11.4%,” and “we returned $6.4 billion to common shareholders, including…repurchases of $5 billion.” (CFO Coleman)

Q&A

  • Glenn Schorr, Evercore ISI: Asked about “balance sheet strategy” and the “trade-off of lower NII… but growing financing”; CFO Coleman: “we significantly expanded our activities in Equities financing,” and “a lot of that growth did derive through the Marcus platform.”
  • Glenn Schorr, Evercore ISI: Asked if new lending will earn ROEs “in line with your long-term goals”; CFO Coleman: “we are generating very attractive returns on that incremental amount of lending activity.”
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: Asked how private credit could affect “growth outlook” and what to watch; CEO Solomon: “we are very confident that we have significant runway to further scale our business toward our $300 billion target.”
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: Asked about AI-driven cyber risk; CEO Solomon: “cybersecurity has long been at the core of our business,” and “we are, therefore, accelerating our investment in” resilience.
  • L. Erika Penala, UBS: Asked about “outlook on the pipeline”; CEO Solomon: “the environment for Investment Banking activity continues to be incredibly robust, particularly M&A activity.”
  • L. Erika Penala, UBS: Asked to confirm private credit opportunity vs losses; CEO Solomon: “to the degree there was a cycle, we'd actually view it as an opportunity,” and CFO Coleman added: “our life-to-date realized losses are 0.”
  • Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo: Asked if higher provisions signal macro concern; CFO Coleman: “the composition…was, in part, attributable to growth,” plus “impairments” and “adjustments for the overall operating environment.”
  • Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo: Asked whether corporates are “more engaged”; CEO Solomon: “people are very engaged,” and “the corporate world…is incredibly engaged right now.”
  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe: Asked about “efficiency outlook” and a “$5 billion baseline”; CFO Coleman: “we remain laser-focused on driving towards the 60% level,” and tied most non-comp increase to “transaction-based expenses.”
  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe: Asked about capital proposals and CET1 comfort; CFO Coleman: “we are encouraged by the direction of travel,” and said “we think that's an appropriate level” at “12.5%.”
  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: Asked about sponsor selling pressure; CEO Solomon: “sponsor activity has been slower,” but “it will turn on.”
  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: Asked about FICC financing exposure to direct lending; CFO Coleman: “we think about it on an underlying asset class perspective,” emphasizing diversification.
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Asked if 32% comp ratio signals full-year direction; CFO Coleman: “32% is our best estimate.”
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Asked about weaker rates/mortgages in FICC; CFO Coleman: “mortgages and rates were lower,” while “currencies and commodities was relatively stronger.”
  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies: Asked about private banking and lending outlook; CFO Coleman: deposit competition “will persist as a headwind for much of 2026,” but “as we move into 2027, we will be back growing that segment.”
  • Devin Ryan, Citizens JMP: Asked which businesses AI impacts most; CEO Solomon: “I am hugely forward leaning on the power of this technology to accelerate growth and efficiency.”
  • Devin Ryan, Citizens JMP: Asked about Asia progress; CFO Coleman: “good progress but more to do across Asia.”
  • Matthew O'Connor, Deutsche Bank: Asked if flows are tracking above the 5% target; CFO Coleman: “First quarter delivered 9%.”
  • Matthew O'Connor, Deutsche Bank: Asked early benefits from T. Rowe/Industry/Innovator; CEO Solomon: “we're integrating the teams,” and “I don't want to overstate it.”
  • Gerard Cassidy, RBC: Asked if leverage-ratio changes helped acquisition financing and hold levels; CFO Coleman: “changes…give us more flexibility,” and “our general philosophy is…underwrite and distribute.”
  • Gerard Cassidy, RBC: Asked about single-name impairments; CFO Coleman: “several very small sort of names…not…thematic.”
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: Asked about CET1 drop and RWA growth; CFO Coleman: “biggest buckets are growth in prime financing, acquisition financing and then market risk RWAs.”
  • Saul Martinez, HSBC: Asked durability of equities revenues; CFO Coleman: drivers include “market caps around the world are expanding” and “balance sheet and capital commitments,” with risk if clients want “a lot less.”
  • Saul Martinez, HSBC: Asked if VaR divergence is just volatility; CFO Coleman: “volatility across rates and commodities…went up,” which “drives the change.”
  • Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo: Asked sponsor share of IB; CEO Solomon: “it's not a number we've disclosed.”
  • Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo: Asked why many companies remain private; CEO Solomon: “the economic incentives…give them optionality to wait.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to pressing, concentrated on capital drawdown (“when the CET drops 180 basis points”), credit provisioning signals (“sending a signal”), and durability of Equities (“durability…what is durable versus what is extraordinary”).
  • Management: slightly positive but risk-aware; Solomon described “a highly dynamic environment,” while reinforcing confidence with phrases like “we are very confident” (private credit runway) and “we remain laser-focused” (efficiency goal).
  • Versus Q4, management’s prepared tone shifted from strategy/targets and optimism (“highly constructive setup for 2026”) to heavier emphasis on uncertainty and volatility drivers in Q1.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Capital stance tightened: CET1 moved from “14.4%” at Q4-end to “12.5%” at Q1-end, with Q1 attribution to “record common stock repurchases of $5 billion” and RWA growth in “prime financing” and “acquisition financing.” (CFO Coleman)
  • Platform Solutions narrative progressed from the Q4 “agreement to transition the Apple Card portfolio” to Q1 reflecting “the move of the Apple portfolio to held for sale” and an expectation that “revenues for the rest of the year” run lower. (CFO Coleman)
  • Strategy continuity: both calls emphasized Asia share gaps and scaling financing; Q1 highlighted execution with “record Equities financing revenues of $2.6 billion.” (CFO Coleman)

Risks and concerns

  • “Heightened uncertainty in parts of private credit” and geopolitical risk from “the conflict in the Middle East” were cited as sentiment headwinds. (Chairman & CEO Solomon)
  • Credit risk reserves and impairments: “Our provision for credit losses of $315 million reflected growth and impairments in our wholesale lending portfolio.” (CFO Coleman) Management stated the build reflected “growth,” “single name impairments,” and “the overall operating environment.” (CFO Coleman)
  • Expense trajectory risk: management tied higher non-comp mainly to “transaction-based expenses,” while also “accelerating…investments in cloud migration” to support AI deployment. (CFO Coleman)

Final takeaway

Goldman Sachs management described Q1 2026 as a quarter of record client activity in Global Banking & Markets alongside higher volatility, highlighting capital deployment into financing businesses (including Asia), continued inflow momentum in Asset & Wealth Management, and an accelerated infrastructure investment agenda to enable AI-driven productivity. Management positioned private credit as a long-term scaling opportunity toward a “$300 billion target,” while emphasizing a lower CET1 ratio as a function of balance sheet growth supporting client activity and “record” shareholder returns through repurchases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Raytheon tests event-based infrared camera for tracking fast-moving targets
data.description:
Raytheon, a business of RTX (RTX), said it demonstrated a new mid-wave infrared camera designed to track high-speed objects in real time while reducing data processing and power requirements.

The company on Monday said the system differs from conventional infrared cameras by using an event-based approach, in which the sensor records changes at the pixel level rather than capturing full image frames. This produces a continuous stream of motion-based data instead of a sequence of images.

During a recent test in Northern California, the camera tracked multiple types of targets, including ground vehicles, aircraft and live-fire activity. According to Raytheon, the system was able to detect rapid motion that can be difficult for traditional frame-based infrared systems to capture, providing near-instantaneous updates during the exercise.

Event-based sensing reduces the amount of data generated by focusing only on changes in a scene, which can lower the computational burden required to process imagery. The approach is being explored as a way to improve response times in environments where large data volumes and fast-moving targets can strain existing systems.

The technology was developed under the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency’s DARPA Fast Event-based Neuromorphic Camera and Electronics (FENCE) program, which aims to advance new sensing architectures inspired by biological vision systems.

Raytheon said potential applications include battlefield monitoring, base protection, missile guidance and airborne or unmanned surveillance, though the system remains in the demonstration phase. The company said it is planning additional testing to evaluate performance across a wider range of scenarios and target types.

data.title: Honeywell technology selected for expansion of Rio Grande LNG project in Texas
data.description:
Honeywell (HON) said it will supply liquefied natural gas process technology and equipment for two additional production units at NextDecade’s Rio Grande LNG export facility in Brownsville, Texas, under an agreement with engineering contractor Bechtel Energy Inc.

The expansion covers Train 4 and Train 5 of the project, where Honeywell (HON) will provide its coil wound heat exchangers and C3MR liquefaction process. The same technology is already slated for use in the facility’s first three trains.

NextDecade said the additional trains are expected to increase total production capacity at the site from 18 million tonnes a year to 30 million tonnes, a rise of more than 66%. All five trains are projected to be operational by mid-2031.

LNG projects are typically built in phases, with each “train” functioning as a separate processing unit that cools natural gas into a liquid form for export. The Rio Grande LNG facility is one of several large-scale export projects under development along the U.S. Gulf Coast.

Honeywell’s (HON) role in the expansion includes supplying key equipment used in the liquefaction process, along with associated technologies aimed at improving plant efficiency and reliability. The company said its systems are designed to support continuous operation and reduce operating costs, though financial terms of the agreement were not disclosed.

The announcement comes as global LNG demand continues to grow, driven in part by efforts to diversify energy supplies and reduce reliance on pipeline gas in some regions. U.S. LNG export capacity has expanded rapidly in recent years, positioning the country as one of the world’s largest suppliers.

Honeywell also said it offers modular LNG systems that can be assembled off-site and transported to export terminals, a method intended to shorten construction timelines compared with traditional on-site builds.

The Rio Grande LNG project remains subject to financing, regulatory approvals, and construction timelines that are typical for large energy infrastructure developments.

data.title: Dollar firms on safe-haven demand amid escalating U.S.-Iran tensions: Currency Recap
data.description:
The U.S. dollar edged up on Monday, as investors turned cautious following renewed geopolitical tensions and a shift back toward safe-haven demand after the collapse of U.S.-Iran peace talks.

The dollar index (DXY), which measures the greenback against a basket of major currencies, was last up 0.14% to $98.79 but remains up about 0.71% for the year to date.

Dollar weekly moves and key drivers:

Over the past week, the U.S. dollar’s moves were driven by a clear sequence of geopolitical developments tied to the U.S.-Iran conflict. Early in the period, the greenback came under pressure as a tentative ceasefire between Washington and Tehran triggered a relief rally in risk assets, prompting investors to unwind safe-haven positions. This weakness extended as optimism around planned peace talks and expectations of easing disruptions to energy flows supported risk-sensitive currencies, putting the dollar on track for its worst weekly performance since January.

However, sentiment remained fragile, and as the week progressed, intermittent doubts over the durability of the truce limited downside in the dollar. Toward the end of the period, the trend reversed sharply, as the collapse of peace talks and renewed threats to energy supply routes, including a potential U.S. blockade of Iranian ports, drove oil prices higher, reignited inflation concerns, and triggered a fresh flight to safety, lifting the dollar.

The rebound in the dollar was further supported by shifting expectations around the global interest rate outlook, as rising energy prices and persistent inflation pressures reduced the likelihood of near-term policy easing by major central banks.

Risk aversion intensified toward the latter part of the week as diplomatic efforts faltered and tensions escalated, including renewed threats to key energy shipping routes such as the Strait of Hormuz. The shift toward a risk-off environment lifted demand for the dollar as a safe-haven currency, while equities came under pressure and energy prices moved higher.

During the period, global markets were influenced by developments in the U.S.-Iran conflict, sharp swings in oil prices, evolving inflation expectations, and broader shifts in risk sentiment.

U.S. Treasury yields were trading low overall, with the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) falling four basis points to 4.31%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) declined eight basis points to 3.79%.

Currency Highlights:

Major currency movements (April 06 to April 12)

 

Euro (EUR:USD)

+1.33%

Pound Sterling (GBP:USD)

+1.35%

Japanese Yen (JPY:USD)

+0.16%

Chinese Yuan (CNY:USD)

+0.72%

Swiss Franc (CHF:USD)

+0.95%

Australian Dollar (AUD:USD)

+1.83%

Canadian Dollar (CAD:USD)

+0.44%

The euro (EUR:USD) was last up 0.09%, with gains supported earlier by optimism around a potential ceasefire that lifted European markets.

However, the currency pared advances as sentiment turned cautious, with European indexes declining amid renewed geopolitical tensions, rising energy prices, and lingering macroeconomic jitters.

The Australian dollar (AUD:USD) was last up 0.34%, reflecting earlier risk-on sentiment that supported growth-linked currencies. However, gains were limited as the currency came under pressure amid renewed geopolitical tensions, with its slide signaling broader risk aversion and potential weakness in U.S. equities.

The Japanese yen (JPY:USD) was last down 0.14%, weakening even as geopolitical tensions escalated, suggesting limited safe-haven demand amid broader dollar strength.

data.title: Food and beverage stocks slump amid fuel price concerns
data.description:
Food and beverage stocks swung lower on Monday as investors focused on the potential impact of rising oil prices. The food sector could see freight, packaging, and other operating costs move higher to squeeze margins. The sector could also be impacted if gas prices in the U.S. remain at an elevated level to cut into spending trends and take away the pricing power of food sellers.

Leading the decline was Conagra Brands (CAG), which fell 5.4% after the company also announced a CEO transition. Other food names trading lower included General Mills (GIS) -3.3%, Seneca Foods (SENEA) -3.2%, Westrock Coffee Company (WEST) -3.1%, JBS N.V. (JBS) -3.1%, Kraft Heinz (KHC) -3.0%, Post Holdings (POST) -2.5%, J.M. Smucker (SJM) -2.5%, Nomad Foods (NOMD) -2.4%, and Campbell's Company (CPB) -2.2%. In the beverage sector, Vita Coco (COCO) -5.2%, Celsius Holdings (CELH) -3.4%, Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) -2.2%, and Coca-Cola (KO) -1.8% were underperforming broad market averages.

Analysts warned that the companies may be cautious with their full-year guidance during the upcoming earnings season due to the geopolitical uncertainty.

data.title: ONEOK reverses Magellan pipeline flows to Texas as exports surge
data.description:
ONEOK (OKE) said late Friday it is currently shipping fuel from Oklahoma to Texas via a segment of its Magellan pipeline, reversing flows that typically carry product northbound to Oklahoma from Texas.

The move comes as the U.S. Gulf Coast has seen fuel inventories dwindle amid a surge in export demand to offset global shortages from the Middle East war.

Jet fuel exports were higher for a fifth straight week, reaching 442K bbl/day, the highest number on record, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration's latest weekly report.

"We work closely with our customers to transport supply to key markets, including the Gulf Coast, while monitoring market developments and making adjustments as needed," a ONEOK (OKE) spokesperson said.

data.title: Greenlight takes new positions in Versant, Crocs, SLM - Q1 letter
data.description:
David Einhorn's Greenlight took new positions in Versant Media (VSNT), Crocs  Inc. (CROX), and SLM Corp. (SLM) in Q1. 

Greenlight initiated a new medium-sized position in Versant (VSNT), which was recently spun off from Comcast and owns channels like MS Now, NBC and USA Network, according to Greenlight's Q1 letter viewed by Seeking Alpha.

"While the legacy cable business faces ongoing cord-cutting, over 60% of its programming is tied to live news and events, which we believe is more resistant to subscriber losses than other entertainment categories," Greenlight wrote. 

Greenlight established its new small position in Crocs (CROX) at $83.49 a share and said concerns about a decline in U.S. sales last year  were "overblown." The shoe company has directed most its free cash flow to buybacks and the hedge fund sees annual repurchase of over 10% outstanding shares going forward. 

As for its new small position in SLM, Greenlight sees "an opportunity for significant growth  in graduate student lending as the federal government exits the market following provisions in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act." 

Greenlight exited its position in Kyndryl (KD) in Q1, as well as positions in Global Payments (GPN) and Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), as the hedge fund believes the auction for the company has reached a resolution. 

The significant winners in the quarter were gold, Acadia Healthcare (ACHC), DHT Holdings (DHT) and Core Natural Resources (CNR). The biggest losers were SOFR futures, Kyndryl (KD( and Graphic Packaging (GPK).

The Greenlight Capital funds returned 6.5% in Q1, net of fees and expenses, compared to -4.4% for the S&P 500 index.

Shares of Crox rose 1.6%, SLM gained 1.7% and Versant ticked higher by 0.8%.

data.title: Starbucks' China franchise deal improves visibility into turnaround -- Jefferies
data.description:
With its China business now franchised, Starbucks (SBUX) has less international exposure. Alongside a stabilized U.S. business, Jefferies upgraded the coffee chain to Hold from Underperform as visibility into its turnaround has “improved.” 

Starbucks’ (SBUX) international business represented ~33% of global system sales, 27% of revenue, and 25% of operating profit prior to the April 2 China transaction, giving the company the least exposure to international markets than McDonald’s (MCD), Yum! Brands (YUM), Restaurant Brands (QSR), and Domino’s Pizza (DPZ).

“We think there is somewhat low visibility into global growth accelerating meaningfully in out years, but the bar/expectations are set low enough,” said Jefferies’ Andy Barish, who models 1.5% net global growth in FY26 and 1.8% in FY27. With the company targeting ~2K global units in FY28 with 1.5K in international markets, this gets the company to 6% to 7% annual growth, while U.S. growth returns to ~2%.

Barish’s neutral rating on Starbucks (SBUX), however, reflects an “unwarranted large premium to its global, asset-lite peers.” Nonetheless, expectations appear finally closer to realistic levels after the last 18 months of optimism since Brian Niccol became CEO.

In addition to an upgrade, Barish raises his target price for Starbucks (SBUX) by 7% to $92, which is a 5% discount to Friday’s closing price.

data.title: Exxon eyes $24B deepwater expansion in Nigeria - report
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (XOM) has unveiled proposals to spend as much as $24B in a pair of long-stalled deepwater oil projects offshore Nigeria, UpstreamOnline.com reported Monday.

Exxon's (XOM) top deepwater official, Hunter Farris, reportedly outlined the company's agenda at a meeting last week with the Nigerian Upstream Petroleum Regulatory Commission officials to spend billions in developing a pair of deepwater projects.

The regulator said the supermajor has been "encouraged" by the country's improved investment climate, so it has decided to "renew our vows to Nigeria," according to the report.

Farris noted that because the production sharing contract covering its the Erha deepwater field had been extended to 2042, the company is now undertaking life extension works to get the Erha floating production, storage, and offloading vessel back to maximum performance.

Production from Erha averaged 63.5K bbl/day in 2025, far short of its capacity of 210K bbl/day, according to NUPRC data.

Exxon (XOM) also is gearing up for "potential" new investments in the 1B-barrel Owowo deepwater project, which will be developed as a subsea tieback to its Usan FPSO, Farris said.

data.title: Goldman CEO says private credit sector still viewed as attractive
data.description:
Goldman Sachs (GS) CEO David Solomon said that his bank continues to view the private credit sector as attractive, despite some "noise."

“We recognize that the private-credit industry has been an area of increased focus in recent months,” Solomon said on the bank's earnings call Monday, according to Bloomberg. “I think there’s going to continue to be some noise around the retail space.”

“This continues, with any sort of a medium-term or longer-term view, to be a very, very attractive platform for us,” Solomon added.

Bloomberg noted a Goldman Sachs private credit fund, Goldman Sachs Private Credit Corp., recently reported that it met redemption requests totaling 4.999% of its outstanding shares during Q1.
